title: Polymer clay Skinner blend technique
subtitle: Creating a smooth gradient sheet by folding and rolling two colours
excerpt: The Skinner blend is a systematic folding and rolling technique for polymer clay that produces a sheet with a smooth gradient from one colour to another. The resulting sheet can be used as is, rolled into a cane, or wrapped around a form.
sourceNotes: The Skinner blend was developed by Judith Skinner and is widely taught in the polymer clay community. Technique described in general terms from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A PVC-based modelling material that cures in a domestic oven. Available in many colours.
Kneading polymer clay thoroughly before use to warm and plasticise it, making it smooth, uniform, and free of air bubbles.

content.text: The Skinner blend is the standard method for creating a smooth colour gradient in
polymer clay

. It works by placing two triangles of different colours on a single sheet, folding the sheet in half lengthwise, and rolling it out again repeatedly. Each fold brings the two colours a step closer together at their shared edge; after twenty to thirty repetitions, the transition is smooth and even.
Conditioning the clay
Before blending, each colour must be thoroughly
conditioned

. Unconditioned polymer clay contains air pockets and uneven plasticiser distribution that produce streaks and cracks rather than a smooth blend. Knead each colour until it is uniformly soft and smooth.
Setting up the blend
Roll each colour to a roughly equal sheet, about 3 mm thick, and cut each into a right-angle triangle using the fettling knife.
Place the two triangles long edge to long edge on the board so they form a rectangle: colour A on the left, colour B on the right.
Press the join between the two triangles together gently.
Running the blend
Roll the rectangle out evenly with the rolling pin to a long thin strip. Keep the rolling direction consistent; always roll lengthwise.
Fold the strip in half with colour A always going back to the left side. Keep the fold consistent: same colour A always ends up on the same side after folding.
Roll out again in the same lengthwise direction.
Repeat the fold and roll sequence 20-30 times. After about ten passes, the two colours begin to blend at the centre. After 25 passes, the gradient should be smooth.
What it looks like when it is right

A finished Skinner blend sheet shows colour A at full saturation at one short end, gradually transitioning through a mixed middle zone to colour B at full saturation at the other end. There should be no hard lines between the colour zones, no patches of unmixed clay, and no streaks.
Using the blend sheet

Cut the final sheet to shape for wrapping around forms, covering pendants, or using as the outer layer of a millefiori cane. Roll from one short end to produce a cane with a gradient from one colour to another along its length. The blend can also be layered into a multi-colour cane by stacking it with other sheets before rolling.
Common mistakes
Changing the fold direction. The fold must always bring the same colour back to the same side. Random folding produces a muddy mix rather than a gradient.
Not conditioning adequately first. Stiff clay tears instead of blending.
Too few repetitions. Twenty passes is the minimum; thirty gives cleaner results.
